skill.name: saga-orchestration
skill.description: Design saga orchestration patterns for distributed transactions, compensating actions, and long-running business workflows.
skill.summary: Build reliable saga workflows with orchestration, choreography, compensation, and timeout patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported command execution, weak crypto, C2, reconnaissance, and network indicators. Manual review found these are Markdown examples, domain words, diagram labels, a UUID import for saga IDs, and documentation links. No executable scripts, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Formatting Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static command findings are inline backticks or fenced Python examples in documentation. They do not execute commands or invoke a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown formatting and Python documentation snippets. No shell API, subprocess call, or runtime script is present.

low_findings.title: Domain Text Misidentified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography alerts are caused by ordinary documentation terms such as design, state, and Data-Intensive. No cryptographic function is implemented or recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is saga design guidance and a book link. There is no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, cipher configuration, or security-sensitive cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Diagram Label Misidentified as C2 Indicator
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword alert occurs inside an architecture diagram that labels services as Svc1, Svc2, and Svc3. It is not command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of a static ASCII diagram comparing choreography and orchestration. It contains no network endpoint, beaconing logic, or control channel.

low_findings.title: UUID Import Misidentified as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert maps to importing uuid for saga identifiers. The example uses UUIDs for workflow correlation, not host fingerprinting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code creates saga IDs with uuid.uuid4. No device identifiers, environment details, usernames, or host metadata are collected.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Misidentified as Network Access
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are resource links for saga pattern reading. The skill does not fetch these URLs or send data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs appear under a Resources heading as Markdown links. There is no runtime HTTP client, request payload, or exfiltration path.

content.value_statement: Distributed transactions fail when services only handle the happy path. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design saga workflows with compensation, retries, and timeouts.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to choose orchestration or choreography for saga workflows.
Provides Python templates for saga state, steps, and orchestrator flow.
Models order fulfillment steps with inventory, payment, shipping, and notification actions.
Shows compensation handlers for failed payment and shipment flows.
Adds timeout handling guidance for long-running saga steps.
Includes a checklist for idempotency keys, correlation IDs, and recovery testing.
limitations: It is documentation and examples, not an installable runtime framework.
Python snippets are illustrative and require adaptation before production use.
It does not provide message broker, database, or scheduler integrations.
It does not verify business-specific compensation correctness.

use_cases.title: Plan Checkout Transactions
use_cases.description: Design an order flow that reserves inventory, charges payment, creates shipment, and compensates failures.

use_cases.title: Review Microservice Failure Paths
use_cases.description: Map service steps, failure events, retries, and rollback actions before implementing a workflow.

use_cases.title: Document Workflow Architecture
use_cases.description: Create clear saga diagrams, state definitions, and operational checks for distributed business processes.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Saga Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Use saga-orchestration to compare orchestration and choreography for this workflow: [describe services and steps]. Recommend one approach and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Saga Steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Use saga-orchestration to define saga steps for [business process]. Include action names, compensation names, state changes, and required correlation data.

prompt_templates.title: Review Failure Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use saga-orchestration to review this workflow for missing compensation, timeout, retry, and idempotency concerns: [paste design summary].

prompt_templates.title: Create an Advanced Orchestrator Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use saga-orchestration to design an orchestrated saga for [workflow]. Include persistence needs, event contracts, timeout policy, recovery behavior, and verification tests.

output_examples.input: Design a saga for order fulfillment across inventory, payment, shipping, and notifications.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step saga plan with actions, compensations, completion events, failure events, and timeout handling.

output_examples.input: Review a choreography-based payment workflow for failure gaps.
output_examples.output: A review that identifies missing rollback events, idempotency requirements, correlation IDs, and duplicate message handling checks.

output_examples.input: Document operational checks for a long-running approval workflow.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering state visibility, operator-visible failures, retry limits, compensation testing, and restart recovery.

best_practices: Define each saga step with a matching compensation action before implementation.
Use idempotency keys and correlation IDs across commands, events, logs, and storage.
Test every failure point, including duplicate messages and orchestrator restart recovery.
anti_patterns: Do not treat a saga as a single atomic database transaction.
Do not publish events without enough context to compensate or resume safely.
Do not skip timeout and retry rules for long-running steps.

faq.question: What does this skill help design?
faq.answer: It helps design saga workflows for distributed transactions, compensation, failure handling, and long-running business processes.

faq.question: Does it provide production-ready code?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and Python examples that teams must adapt, test, and integrate.

faq.question: When should I choose orchestration?
faq.answer: Choose orchestration when centralized control, visibility, and explicit workflow state are important.

faq.question: When should I choose choreography?
faq.answer: Choose choreography when services are autonomous and event coupling remains manageable.

faq.question: Can it help with compensation design?
faq.answer: Yes. It prompts you to define rollback actions, failure events, and tests for each step.

faq.question: Does the skill require network access?
faq.answer: No. The only URLs are documentation references in the resources section.
